topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight. My stomach clenched immediately, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just below my ribs. It was from Sarah, the supposed mastermind behind the most elaborate surprise celebration I’d ever imagined. The subject line read simply: "Update Regarding the Party." Reading the terse message—stating that due to 'unforeseen circumstances,' the event was officially scrubbed—felt like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, my hands trembling slightly as I reread the words, searching for any hidden meaning, any justification that made sense. A slow, cold burn started in my chest, and I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white. I couldn't bring myself to call anyone; the silence of the realization was deafening, amplifying the sharp, stinging feeling of being completely dismissed.

[story 2]

I walked into the living room, the cheerful decorations—the streamers, the carefully arranged snacks—hanging limp and forlorn. The air felt thick and strangely hollow. When I saw Mark standing by the door, looking apologetic but utterly devoid of warmth, my jaw tightened. "So, it’s off?" I managed, my voice coming out flat, stripped bare of any pretense of happiness. I watched him shift his weight, a subtle movement that spoke volumes about his reluctance. Every polite smile I tried to muster felt brittle and false. I took a slow, deliberate breath, forcing my shoulders back, trying to keep the tremor in my hands from showing. I didn't need to say anything more; the disappointment radiated off me in palpable waves, making the space around me feel suddenly too large and cold.

[story 3]

My phone screen illuminated my face, displaying the cancellation notice. For a long moment, nothing moved. Then, a sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I dropped the phone onto the sofa cushion as if it had burned me. The carefully constructed anticipation I had nurtured for weeks evaporated, leaving behind a residue of bitter frustration. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, my movements sharp and jerky. I stopped by the window, staring out at the street, feeling a profound sense of being overlooked, as if my entire effort and excitement had been treated as utterly inconsequential. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat—a sound of pure, contained exasperation that vibrated in my chest.
